methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series B → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.9x) to $220M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 6 same-sector + same/adjacent-stage peers in Chips & Semiconductors. Range: $500M - $5.0B.

methods.name: Public-Comp Anchoring
methods.skip_reason: target revenue not on file (private companies rarely disclose)
methods.rationale: Public Semiconductors P/S median is 20.3x across 3 tickers. Cannot apply without target's revenue.

private.name: Etched
private.rationale: Same sector (Chips & Semiconductors), same stage (Series B), post-money $5B

private.name: Positron AI
private.rationale: Same sector (Chips & Semiconductors), same stage (Series B), post-money $1B+

data.narrative:
Fractile is a Series B-stage semiconductor company founded by Walter Goodwin that has raised $220M to date. The valuation assessment draws on a peer universe of 8 private and 3 public semiconductor companies, including same-stage comparables like Etched ($5.0B), Positron AI ($1.0B), and adjacent-stage peers such as d-Matrix ($2.0B Series C) and DEEPX ($529M Series C).

Two triangulation methods are applicable. Stage-progression analysis applies a 1.9x multiplier to the $220M total raised, implying a valuation of $418M. Peer-median analysis across the 6 same-sector, same/adjacent-stage private comparables yields a median valuation of $1.0B, with a range spanning $500M to $5.0B. Public-comp anchoring cannot be applied due to the absence of Fractile's revenue figures, which private companies rarely disclose. Public semiconductor median multiples are 20.3x P/S across ALAB, NVDA, and AMD, but lack of revenue data prevents calibration.

Synthesizing the two applicable methods produces an implied range of $355M (low) to $1.1B (high), with a median of $709M. The rumored $1.0B valuation aligns with the peer-median point estimate and falls within the high end of the triangulated range. Key uncertainties include the wide dispersion across peer valuations, reflecting stage and business-model variance within the semiconductor frontier-tech cohort, and the complete absence of revenue visibility needed to anchor public-market multiples.
